title: Simulation & Management: Systems, Ticks, Economy & Sandbox
html:
Games that run themselves

Simulation and management games — think colony sims, tycoons, city builders, factory games — are different from everything you have built so far. Instead of one player avatar reacting frame-by-frame, you author a world of autonomous agents and interacting systems that keep evolving whether the player touches them or not. The player's job is to nudge the system: build a farm, set a tax rate, assign a worker. The fun lives in the emergent feedback loops.

The single most important architectural decision in a sim is the tick. Real-time games update in Update() every frame, but a sim's logic should advance on a fixed, framerate-independent cadence — say four ticks per second — so behavior is deterministic, pausable, and fast-forwardable. You decouple simulation time from render time.

A central tick driver

Build one authority that owns time. Everything else subscribes to it. This keeps the whole sim in lockstep and gives you pause and speed controls for free.

SimClock.cs
// One driver owns simulation time. Systems subscribe to OnTick. using System; using UnityEngine; public class SimClock : MonoBehaviour { public static SimClock Instance { get; private set; } public event Action OnTick; // fired once per simulated tick [SerializeField] float ticksPerSecond = 4f; public float Speed = 1f; // 0 = paused, 2 = fast-forward float _accum; public int TickCount { get; private set; } void Awake() { Instance = this; } void Update() { if (Speed <= 0f) return; // paused: no ticks emitted _accum += Time.deltaTime * Speed; float step = 1f / ticksPerSecond; while (_accum >= step) { // catch up if a frame was slow _accum -= step; TickCount++; OnTick?.Invoke(); } } }
Why a while-loop.
If one frame hangs for 300ms, a single if would emit one tick and silently drop simulated time. The while drains the accumulator so the sim stays correct after hitches. This is the same fixed-timestep pattern Unity's own FixedUpdate uses internally.

An economy as a system

An economy is just resources that flow between producers and consumers each tick. Model resources as data, not as scattered fields. A simple Stockpile plus a few IProducer agents gives you a living economy.

Economy.cs
using System.Collections.Generic; using UnityEngine; public enum Resource { Food, Wood, Gold } public class Stockpile : MonoBehaviour { readonly Dictionary<Resource,int> _amounts = new(); public int Get(Resource r) => _amounts.TryGetValue(r, out var v) ? v : 0; public void Add(Resource r, int n) => _amounts[r] = Get(r) + n; public bool TrySpend(Resource r, int n) { if (Get(r) < n) return false; _amounts[r] -= n; return true; } } // An agent that turns one resource into another every few ticks. public class Workshop : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] Stockpile pile; [SerializeField] int everyTicks = 4; void OnEnable() { SimClock.Instance.OnTick += Tick; } void OnDisable() { if (SimClock.Instance) SimClock.Instance.OnTick -= Tick; } void Tick() { if (SimClock.Instance.TickCount % everyTicks != 0) return; // recipe: 2 Wood -> 1 Gold if (pile.TrySpend(Resource.Wood, 2)) pile.Add(Resource.Gold, 1); } }

Agent behavior on the tick

Agents (workers, citizens, machines) should run a tiny state machine driven by the tick, not by Update. A worker that walks to a tree, chops it, and returns wood is just three states advanced once per tick. Because everything keys off SimClock, setting Speed = 0 freezes the entire world instantly, and Speed = 3 fast-forwards production, agent movement, and the economy together — no special-casing needed.

Determinism check.
A true simulation should produce the same result from the same starting state and tick count, regardless of framerate. Avoid Random seeded by time, avoid reading Time.deltaTime inside tick logic, and you can later add replays, network sync, and reliable saves almost for free.

Sandbox feel

What makes a management game a sandbox is that the player edits the world: placing buildings on a grid (reuse your Snake-era grid math from lesson p2-5), zoning areas, and watching consequences ripple. Keep the player's tools (placement, demolition) on the input/render side, and keep consequences (production, growth, decay) on the tick side. That clean split is the secret to a sim that scales from ten agents to ten thousand.

🎮 Build This: Simulation / Management — Tiny Tycoon

Genre: simulation / management. This STARTS a new genre — your first tick-driven sandbox, though it reuses the grid math from Snake (p2-5) and ScriptableObject data ideas from p2-11.

  1. Create a SimClock GameObject and add the script above; expose pause (Speed 0), play (1), and fast-forward (2) buttons on a small uGUI HUD (reuse p2-13 skills).
  2. Add a Stockpile and seed it with 20 Wood. Drop two Workshop agents that convert Wood to Gold on different tick intervals.
  3. Build a click-to-place system on a grid: clicking an empty cell spawns a Workshop prefab (p2-4 spawning) and shows its recipe.
  4. Display Food/Wood/Gold totals on the HUD, refreshed each tick, so the player watches the economy breathe.
  5. Add one feedback loop: a Farm agent that produces Food, and citizens that consume 1 Food per tick — if Food hits zero, growth stops. Now you have a real, interacting economy.
  6. Prove determinism: log a checksum of all resources at tick 100. Run at speed 1 and speed 4 — the checksum should match.

title: Performance: Profiler, Memory, Draw Calls & Optimization
html:
Measure first, optimize second

The cardinal rule of optimization: never guess. Programmers waste days hand-optimizing code that was never the bottleneck. Unity ships world-class measurement tools — the Profiler, the Frame Debugger, and the Memory Profiler — and your job in this lesson is to learn to read them, find the real cost, and fix it.

A game runs at 60 FPS only if every frame finishes in 16.6 milliseconds (1000/60). At 30 FPS you have 33.3ms. That budget is split between CPU work (scripts, physics, animation) and GPU work (rendering). Whichever side blows the budget is your bottleneck — and they are fixed in completely different ways.

Reading the Profiler

Open Window > Analysis > Profiler, enter Play mode, and watch the timeline. The CPU Usage track is your map: tall spikes are expensive frames. Click a frame and switch the bottom panel to Hierarchy to see exactly which methods cost what. Two columns matter most:

ColumnWhat it tells you
Time ms / Self msTotal vs. this-method-only cost. High Self ms = the hot spot is right here.
GC AllocBytes of garbage allocated this frame. Anything nonzero in Update is a future stutter.
The GC spike.
Managed allocations are not free — they accrue until the garbage collector runs, which can freeze a frame for several milliseconds. A game that allocates 2KB per frame will hitch periodically. The goal in steady-state gameplay is zero GC Alloc per frame.

Killing per-frame allocations

The most common offenders are easy to spot once you know them. Here is allocating code beside its zero-alloc rewrite.

HotPath.cs
// BAD — allocates every single frame void Update() { GameObject[] enemies = GameObject.FindGameObjectsWithTag("Enemy"); // new array + scan foreach (var e in enemies) { ... } transform.position += new Vector3(0,0,1) * Time.deltaTime; // fine (struct) but... Debug.Log("pos " + transform.position); // string concat = garbage } // GOOD — cache once, reuse buffers, avoid string building [SerializeField] Enemy[] enemies; // assigned once in Awake or the editor static readonly RaycastHit[] _hits = new RaycastHit[16]; // reusable buffer void Update() { for (int i = 0; i < enemies.Length; i++) { /* iterate cached, index-based */ } // NonAlloc physics writes into the existing buffer instead of returning a new array int n = Physics.RaycastNonAlloc(transform.position, Vector3.forward, _hits, 50f); }

Other big wins: cache GetComponent results in Awake (never call it in Update), avoid LINQ in hot paths (it allocates iterators and closures), and use CompareTag("X") instead of tag == "X" which allocates a string.

Draw calls and the Frame Debugger

If the GPU is your bottleneck, the culprit is usually too many draw calls — each unique material/mesh combination the GPU must set up separately. Open Window > Analysis > Frame Debugger, click Enable, and step through every draw call in the frame. You will see precisely what is drawn and why a batch broke (different material, different shader keyword, etc.).

  • Static batching: mark non-moving objects as Static so Unity merges them.
  • GPU instancing: enable it on materials used by many identical objects (rocks, bullets, trees) to draw them in one call.
  • SRP Batcher: with URP, keep shaders compatible so Unity binds material data efficiently.
  • Share materials: every unique material is a potential batch break. Atlas your textures.

Measuring a specific block

To time a suspect section precisely, wrap it in a ProfilerMarker — it shows up as a labeled row in the Profiler Hierarchy.

CustomMarker.cs
using Unity.Profiling; public class Pathfinder : MonoBehaviour { static readonly ProfilerMarker _marker = new("Pathfinder.Solve"); void Solve() { using (_marker.Auto()) { // shows as 'Pathfinder.Solve' in the Profiler // ...expensive pathfinding work here... } } }
Optimization law.
Profile on the target device, not just the editor — a build on real hardware behaves very differently. Optimize the biggest spike, re-measure, repeat. Stop when you hit your frame budget; over-optimizing readable code that is already fast enough is its own kind of waste.
🎮 Build This: Optimization Lab — Stress & Fix

Genre: optimization concept lesson (no new game). You will profile and repair one of your existing games — ideally the Twin-Stick Shooter (p2-15) or Tiny Tycoon (p2-25), which spawn many objects.

  1. Open the Profiler, enter Play mode, and deliberately spawn hundreds of agents/bullets until the frame time climbs above 16.6ms.
  2. Find the worst frame, open Hierarchy, sort by Self ms, and identify the top method. Note its GC Alloc.
  3. Eliminate per-frame allocations: cache components, swap FindGameObjectsWithTag for a cached list, and replace any returning physics calls with NonAlloc variants.
  4. Open the Frame Debugger and count draw calls. Enable GPU instancing on the bullet/agent material and confirm the count drops.
  5. Wrap your most expensive custom method in a ProfilerMarker and verify the cost moved after your fix.
  6. Document before/after frame time and GC Alloc — that delta is your proof the optimization mattered.

title: Asset Pipeline & Addressables: Importing, Bundling & Loading
html:
From source file to runtime asset

Every texture, model, and audio clip you drop into a Unity project goes through the asset pipeline: Unity imports the source file, applies an importer with settings you control, and writes an optimized internal representation. Understanding this pipeline is the difference between a 4GB bloated build and a lean, fast-loading one.

Each asset has an importer inspector. For a texture you choose its Max Size, compression format, and whether it is a sprite, normal map, or UI element. For audio you choose load type (decompress on load vs. streaming) and compression. These settings are stored in a .meta file beside the asset — which is why you must commit .meta files to version control. Lose them and Unity re-imports with defaults, breaking references.

Texture sizes matter most.
Textures dominate build size and memory. A single uncompressed 4096x4096 texture is ~64MB in RAM. Cap Max Size at what the asset actually needs on screen, and use platform-appropriate compression (ASTC on mobile, BC7/DXT on desktop). This one habit routinely cuts build size in half.

Automating import with a postprocessor

Setting importer options by hand for hundreds of assets is error-prone. An AssetPostprocessor runs automatically whenever an asset is imported, letting you enforce project standards in code.

TextureImportRules.cs
// Place in an Editor/ folder. Runs on every texture import. using UnityEditor; public class TextureImportRules : AssetPostprocessor { void OnPreprocessTexture() { // Enforce sane defaults for anything under Assets/UI if (!assetPath.Contains("/UI/")) return; var importer = (TextureImporter)assetImporter; importer.textureType = TextureImporterType.Sprite; importer.mipmapEnabled = false; // UI never needs mipmaps importer.maxTextureSize = 1024; importer.textureCompression = TextureImporterCompression.Compressed; } }

The problem Addressables solves

The naive way to load assets is to put them in a scene or a Resources/ folder. But everything in a scene loads when the scene loads, and everything in Resources/ loads into memory at startup and bloats your build. Neither scales. Addressables is Unity's modern solution: you tag any asset with an address string, and load it asynchronously, on demand, then release it when done. Memory is held only while you actually need the asset.

Install the Addressables package, then mark assets Addressable in the inspector (a checkbox appears) and give them an address like "enemies/boss". Now load by address — the system fetches from a local bundle or even a remote server, transparently.

BossSpawner.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.AddressableAssets; using UnityEngine.ResourceManagement.AsyncOperations; public class BossSpawner : MonoBehaviour { AsyncOperationHandle<GameObject> _handle; public async void SpawnBoss() { // Async load by address; await without blocking the main thread _handle = Addressables.LoadAssetAsync<GameObject>("enemies/boss"); GameObject prefab = await _handle.Task; if (_handle.Status == AsyncOperationStatus.Succeeded) Instantiate(prefab, transform.position, Quaternion.identity); } void OnDestroy() { // CRITICAL: release the handle or the asset stays in memory forever if (_handle.IsValid()) Addressables.Release(_handle); } }
Always release.
Addressables uses reference counting. Every LoadAssetAsync must be balanced by a Release (or Addressables.ReleaseInstance for instantiated objects). Forgetting is the number-one Addressables bug — a slow memory leak that only shows up after long play sessions.

Instantiating and releasing in one step

For prefabs you spawn and later destroy, InstantiateAsync pairs cleanly with ReleaseInstance, handling both the GameObject and its reference count.

SpawnRelease.cs
var handle = Addressables.InstantiateAsync("enemies/grunt", pos, Quaternion.identity); GameObject grunt = await handle.Task; // later, when the grunt dies: Addressables.ReleaseInstance(grunt); // destroys it AND decrements the ref count

Content delivery

Because Addressables build into bundles, you can host those bundles on a CDN and ship updated content — new levels, seasonal items, balance tweaks — without rebuilding the whole game. The player downloads only the changed bundle. This is how live-service games push content. For a first project you will build bundles locally, but the same address-based code works unchanged when you flip to remote hosting later.

Pipeline law.
Decide load strategy per asset: bake small, always-needed things into the scene; make large or optional things (bosses, levels, cosmetics) Addressable; never dump everything in Resources/. The pipeline is a budgeting tool — spend memory and build size only where the player benefits.
🎮 Build This: Asset Pipeline — On-Demand Content

Genre: asset-pipeline concept lesson (no new game). You will retrofit Addressables into a prior game — the FPS (p2-16) or RPG (p2-20) are ideal because they have heavy assets like enemy prefabs and levels.

  1. Install the Addressables package and write the TextureImportRules postprocessor; drop a texture into Assets/UI and confirm its importer settings change automatically.
  2. Mark a heavy prefab (a boss, a weapon, or a whole level) as Addressable and give it a clear address.
  3. Replace its direct reference with the async BossSpawner pattern, loading by address only when the player reaches that encounter.
  4. Add an OnDestroy release (or use ReleaseInstance) and verify in the Profiler that memory drops after the asset is released.
  5. Build the Addressables groups, run the build, and confirm the asset is bundled separately from your main scenes.
  6. Bonus: point the group's load path at a local web server URL to simulate downloadable content with no code changes.

title: Netcode for GameObjects: Multiplayer Basics & Networking
html:
Two machines, one shared world

Multiplayer is the moment your game stops living in one process. Netcode for GameObjects (NGO) is Unity's official high-level networking package. It synchronizes GameObjects across a network so that a player on one machine and a player on another see — and agree on — the same world. This lesson takes your First-Person Shooter (p2-16) and makes it online.

NGO uses a client-server model. One machine is the server (the authority that owns the true game state); others are clients (they send input and render what the server tells them). In a host setup, one machine is both server and a client at once — perfect for testing and for peer-hosted games.

Server authority is law.
Never trust the client. The client may be hacked, lagging, or lying. The server decides who took damage, who picked up the loot, and where everyone really is. Clients request; the server decides and broadcasts the result. Get this wrong and your game is trivially cheatable.

The three building blocks

ConceptRole
NetworkManagerThe hub: starts host/server/client, manages connections and the network scene.
NetworkObjectMarks a prefab/object as network-synced; gives it a unique ID across machines.
NetworkBehaviourReplaces MonoBehaviour for networked scripts; unlocks RPCs and NetworkVariable.

Syncing state with NetworkVariable

A NetworkVariable<T> is a value the server owns and every client automatically receives. Change it on the server, and every client's copy updates over the wire — no manual messaging.

NetPlayerHealth.cs
using Unity.Netcode; using UnityEngine; public class NetPlayerHealth : NetworkBehaviour { // Server writes, everyone reads. Auto-replicated. public NetworkVariable<int> Health = new( 100, NetworkVariableReadPermission.Everyone, NetworkVariableWritePermission.Server); public override void OnNetworkSpawn() { Health.OnValueChanged += (oldV, newV) => { // runs on every client when the server changes Health Debug.Log($"Health is now {newV}"); }; } // Only the server should ever call this. public void ApplyDamage(int amount) { if (!IsServer) return; Health.Value = Mathf.Max(0, Health.Value - amount); } }

RPCs: crossing the wire on purpose

When you need to do something rather than mirror a value, you call an RPC (Remote Procedure Call). A ServerRpc runs a client's request on the server; a ClientRpc runs a server's command on clients. This is exactly how a hitscan shot from your FPS should work: the client asks to fire, the server validates and resolves the raycast, then tells everyone the result.

NetShooter.cs
using Unity.Netcode; using UnityEngine; public class NetShooter : NetworkBehaviour { [SerializeField] float range = 100f; [SerializeField] int damage = 25; void Update() { // Only the owning client reads its own input if (!IsOwner) return; if (Input.GetButtonDown("Fire1")) FireServerRpc(transform.position, transform.forward); } // Client -> Server: a REQUEST to fire, validated server-side [ServerRpc] void FireServerRpc(Vector3 origin, Vector3 dir) { if (Physics.Raycast(origin, dir, out var hit, range)) { var hp = hit.collider.GetComponentInParent<NetPlayerHealth>(); if (hp) hp.ApplyDamage(damage); // server-authoritative damage } SpawnTracerClientRpc(origin, dir); // tell everyone to draw the shot } // Server -> all Clients: cosmetic effect, runs everywhere [ClientRpc] void SpawnTracerClientRpc(Vector3 origin, Vector3 dir) { Debug.DrawRay(origin, dir * range, Color.yellow, 0.1f); } }
Ownership and IsOwner.
Each networked object has an owner. Guard input with if (!IsOwner) return; so a player only controls their character, not everyone else's. Forgetting this means every client tries to drive every player — a classic beginner chaos bug.

Spawning networked objects

You cannot just Instantiate a networked prefab — the other machines would not know about it. Instantiate on the server, then call GetComponent<NetworkObject>().Spawn(). NGO replicates it to all clients with a matching ID. Player objects are handled for you when you set a Player Prefab on the NetworkManager.

Transport & testing.
NGO runs over the Unity Transport package. To test locally, build a copy and run host + client side by side, or use Multiplayer Play Mode to launch multiple virtual players from one editor. Start with a host and one client before worrying about dedicated servers or relays.
🎮 Build This: Online Multiplayer — FPS Goes Networked

Genre: online multiplayer. This EXTENDS your First-Person Shooter from p2-16 — same controller and hitscan weapon, now server-authoritative and shared across machines.

  1. Install Netcode for GameObjects and Unity Transport. Add a NetworkManager with Unity Transport and assign a Player Prefab (your FPS character with a NetworkObject).
  2. Add a tiny UI with Host / Client buttons that call NetworkManager.Singleton.StartHost() and StartClient().
  3. Convert the player's movement and shooter scripts to NetworkBehaviour; guard input with if (!IsOwner) return;.
  4. Move health to a server-write NetworkVariable<int> (the NetPlayerHealth above) and resolve the hitscan via FireServerRpc so damage is authoritative.
  5. Broadcast the tracer with a ClientRpc so both players see the shot.
  6. Build a copy and run host + client (or use Multiplayer Play Mode). Confirm two players can move, shoot each other, and see synchronized health.

title: The Build Pipeline: Platforms, WebGL, Settings & Shipping
html:
Turning a project into a product

A game that only runs in the editor is not shipped. The build pipeline compiles your scripts, packages your assets, and produces a standalone executable, an app, or a WebGL bundle that real players can run. This lesson takes any of your games from "works on my machine" to a polished, distributable build.

Builds start in File > Build Settings (or Build Profiles in recent versions). Two things matter here: the scene list (which scenes are included and in what order — index 0 is your first loaded scene, usually a main menu) and the target platform (Windows, macOS, Linux, WebGL, mobile). Switching platform re-imports assets for that target, so pick early.

The empty-build trap.
If a scene is not in the Scenes In Build list, it will not exist in the build — SceneManager.LoadScene will fail at runtime even though it worked in the editor. The number-one shipping bug is forgetting to add scenes. Check this list first, every time.

Player Settings: your game's identity

Edit > Project Settings > Player is where the build gets its identity and behavior. The essentials to set before any release:

SettingWhy it matters
Company & Product NameUsed in the window title, save paths, and store metadata.
IconThe app icon players see on desktop and in stores — set it or you ship the default Unity logo.
Resolution & window modeFullscreen vs. windowed, default resolution, allowed resolutions.
Scripting Backend (IL2CPP vs Mono)IL2CPP compiles to C++ for faster, harder-to-decompile builds; required for WebGL and most consoles.
Managed Stripping LevelRemoves unused code to shrink the build; higher levels need testing.

Compression and build size

Players judge a download by its size, and stores often cap it. Set Compression Method (LZ4 for fast loads, LZ4HC for smaller builds), strip unused code, and remember the texture-import discipline from lesson p2-27 — textures are usually the bulk of your size. Use the build's Build Report (and the Build Profiles size breakdown) to see exactly what is eating space.

WebGL: shipping in the browser

WebGL is special — it runs your game in any modern browser with no install, which is perfect for portfolios and itch.io. But it has constraints: no threads by default, longer startup, and tight memory limits. Three settings make or break a WebGL build:

  • Compression Format: Brotli gives the smallest download but needs server config; Gzip is a safe default; Disabled is largest but works anywhere.
  • Memory size: set a sane heap; too low and the page crashes, too high and mobile browsers refuse it.
  • Decompression fallback: enable it if you cannot configure the web server's content-encoding headers (common on static hosts like Cloudflare Pages or GitHub Pages).
Test the actual build, not the editor.
WebGL behaves very differently from the editor — input, audio autoplay restrictions, and timing all change in a browser. Always run the real build locally before publishing. After a WebGL build, serve it over HTTP (not file://) or the loader will fail.

Scripting and automating builds

For the final ship you usually click Build, but you can also build from code — essential for CI, nightly builds, or one-button multi-platform exports.

BuildScript.cs
// Editor/ folder. Menu item: Build > Build Windows using UnityEditor; using UnityEditor.Build.Reporting; using UnityEngine; public static class BuildScript { [MenuItem("Build/Build Windows")] public static void BuildWindows() { var options = new BuildPlayerOptions { scenes = new[] { "Assets/Scenes/Menu.unity", "Assets/Scenes/Game.unity" }, locationPathName = "Builds/Windows/MyGame.exe", target = BuildTarget.StandaloneWindows64, options = BuildOptions.None }; BuildReport report = BuildPipeline.BuildPlayer(options); var s = report.summary; if (s.result == BuildResult.Succeeded) Debug.Log($"Build OK: {s.totalSize} bytes in {s.totalTime}"); else Debug.LogError($"Build failed: {s.totalErrors} errors"); } }
Shipping checklist.
Before you call a build done: all scenes in the list, icon and product name set, a main menu and a quit button, builds run on a clean machine (no editor assets implied), no debug logs spamming, and the version number bumped. A shipped build is a promise that it runs without you sitting next to it.
🎮 Build This: Shipping — Export a Playable Build

Genre: shipping concept lesson (no new game). You will package any finished prior game — the platformer (p2-7) or twin-stick shooter (p2-15) make great WebGL portfolio pieces.

  1. Open Build Settings, add your menu scene at index 0 and your gameplay scene after it; remove any test scenes.
  2. In Player Settings, set Company/Product name, assign an icon, and choose a default resolution and window mode.
  3. Build a desktop standalone with LZ4HC compression. Run it on your machine outside the editor and confirm scene transitions and the quit button work.
  4. Switch the platform to WebGL, set Compression Format to Gzip with decompression fallback on, and build again.
  5. Serve the WebGL build over a local HTTP server and test it in a browser — verify input, audio, and load time.
  6. Add the BuildScript menu item and run a one-click Windows build to prove you can automate the pipeline.

title: Capstone: Ship a Polished, Multi-System Game of Your Choice
html:
Everything, in one game

This is the finale. Across this track you built a sandbox sim, a first-person shooter, a third-person shooter with NavMesh AI, a 3D racer, a platformer, an RPG, a tower defense, and more — and you learned the systems that hold games together: ScriptableObject architecture, the new Input System, the Animator, Cinemachine, save systems, UI, audio, VFX, networking, profiling, and the build pipeline. The capstone asks you to combine these into one cohesive, shipped game that you would proudly put at the top of a portfolio.

You choose the genre. Pick something you can actually finish and polish in a focused timeframe — a vertical slice done excellently beats a sprawling game done poorly. The grading rubric of the real world is simple: does it run on someone else's machine, does it feel good, and does it show a range of systems working together?

Scope first, code second

The number-one reason capstones fail is scope. Define your minimum shippable game on paper before opening Unity: one core loop, one win condition, one lose condition. Everything else is a stretch goal you add only if time remains.

The vertical slice law.
Build one level, one enemy type, one weapon — but make all of it shippable: animated, juiced, saved, audio-mixed, and bug-free. A polished slice demonstrates more skill than ten half-built levels. Cut features ruthlessly; polish what survives.

A capstone systems checklist

A portfolio-defining game weaves multiple systems. Aim to demonstrate at least five of these, integrated — not bolted on:

SystemFrom lessonWhat it proves
Decoupled architecturep2-11 (ScriptableObjects, events)You can build maintainable, scalable code.
AI / pathfindingp2-17 (NavMesh) or p2-12 (state machines)You can make a world that reacts.
UI & menusp2-13 (uGUI)Your game is approachable and complete.
Save / loadp2-14 (JSON persistence)Progress survives a restart.
Audio, VFX, game feelp2-24 (mixer, particles, juice)Your game feels good to play.
Performance & buildp2-26, p2-29It runs well and actually ships.

A thin core-loop spine

Whatever the genre, a clean GameManager state machine ties the systems together: it owns the high-level states (menu, playing, paused, win, lose) and raises events the rest of the game listens to. Build this spine early and hang features off it.

GameManager.cs
using System; using UnityEngine; public enum GameState { Menu, Playing, Paused, Win, Lose } public class GameManager : MonoBehaviour { public static GameManager Instance { get; private set; } public event Action<GameState> OnStateChanged; // UI, audio, AI subscribe GameState _state = GameState.Menu; public GameState State => _state; void Awake() { if (Instance && Instance != this) { Destroy(gameObject); return; } Instance = this; DontDestroyOnLoad(gameObject); } public void SetState(GameState next) { if (next == _state) return; _state = next; Time.timeScale = (next == GameState.Paused) ? 0f : 1f; OnStateChanged?.Invoke(next); // one event drives the whole game } public void Win() => SetState(GameState.Win); public void Lose() => SetState(GameState.Lose); }
Let systems react, don't poll.
Your HUD, music layer, and AI should each subscribe to OnStateChanged and respond, rather than checking the state every frame. This is the decoupled, event-driven architecture from p2-11 paying off — adding a new reaction means adding one subscriber, touching no existing code.

Ship it for real

A capstone is not done until someone else can play it. Run the full shipping checklist from p2-29: every scene in the build list, an icon and product name, a real main menu and quit button, no console spam, and a profiled, smooth frame rate from p2-26. Export a WebGL build and put it where people can click it — itch.io, your portfolio site, or Cloudflare Pages. A link a recruiter can open in one click is worth more than any screenshot.

Reflect and document.
Write a short README: what genre it is, which systems you used, what you would do next. Articulating your own architecture is a senior-level skill — and it turns a project into a story you can tell in an interview.
🎮 Build This: Capstone — Your Portfolio Game

Genre: your choice. This EXTENDS any prior game — pick the one you most enjoyed (FPS, third-person + AI, racer, RPG, sim, tower defense, platformer) and elevate it to a finished vertical slice, or remix two into something new.

  1. Write a one-page design: core loop, win/lose conditions, and the five-plus systems you will integrate from the table above.
  2. Build the GameManager spine first; wire menu, playing, paused, win, and lose states with events.
  3. Implement the core loop end to end with one of everything: one level, one enemy, one objective — fully playable start to finish.
  4. Layer in persistence (p2-14), audio + VFX + juice (p2-24), and a complete UI/menu flow (p2-13).
  5. Profile and fix your worst frame (p2-26); confirm a steady frame rate and zero per-frame GC in steady state.
  6. Run the full shipping checklist (p2-29), export desktop and WebGL builds, test on a clean machine, and publish the link.
  7. Write a README documenting the genre, systems, and architecture. You have now shipped a complete Unity game — congratulations.
